one_line_takeaway: A statistics paper proving that a simple Hessian-regularized fine-tuning estimator (ULS) is minimax-optimal for approximating a retrained model after data removal using only the forget set and a small subsample of retained data — and, as a side result, that the popular LLM-unlearning heuristic GradDiff is provably sub-optimal by comparison — but it explicitly targets bias-correction/outlier-removal, not privacy/security-certified unlearning.
key_finding: Theorem 3.1 decomposes ULS's estimation error into an oracle rate O(sqrt(p/N_r)) plus an unlearning cost O(omega_f * delta * sqrt(p/tilde n_r)), where omega_f is the forget-data proportion and delta = ||theta_r - theta_f||_2 is the model discrepancy between forget and remaining data distributions. Theorem 3.3 proves this rate is minimax optimal when omega_f*delta <= 1; the robustified ULS+ (Theorem 5.2) is minimax optimal unconditionally. Theorem 5.3 proves GradDiff's convergence rate only matches the (slower) subsampled-OLS rate O(sqrt(p/tilde n_r)) even under optimally tuned lambda, i.e., GradDiff is NOT minimax optimal, unlike ULS. Simulations (Nr=20000, Nf=1000/2000, p in {10,50,100}, delta in {1,2,3}, subsample ratio tilde n_r/N_r in {0.1,0.2,0.3}) show ULS estimation error tracking the retrained oracle theta_hat_r^(rtr) closely and outperforming theta_hat_p, GradDiff, and OLS across all settings; with cross-validated tuning, GradDiff's error becomes 'virtually indistinguishable' from OLS. Inference simulations (Table 1, Nr=20000, Nf=1000) show ULS's 95% CI coverage close to nominal (e.g., 0.935-0.960 across subsample ratios/p/delta) versus OLS (0.942-0.960), but ULS's average standard deviation is consistently smaller (e.g., 0.73-0.81 x1e-2 for ULS vs. 1.30-2.26 x1e-2 for OLS). On the Yelp review dataset (Nr=129,144, Nf=38,569, p=1500 bag-of-words features, tilde n_r/N_r=0.1), ULS/ULS+ prediction accuracy tracks the retrained oracle while running in 0.25s (ULS) / 25.20s (ULS+, incl. CV) versus 0.44s (retrain), 90.08s (PRU), 25.60s (GradDiff, incl. CV), and 0.12s (OLS) on a 48-core Xeon Gold 5418Y; PRU is reported as ~300x slower than ULS despite comparable accuracy when Nf is large. On UK Biobank hospital length-of-stay data (Nr=5619, Nf=577, p=16, IQR outlier threshold of 38 days), ULS/ULS+ again show lowest prediction error among baselines, and for the 'one or more operative procedures' coefficient ULS's 95% CIs are narrower than OLS's across subsample ratios (e.g., at tilde n_r/N_r=0.1: ULS 0.158+/-0.029 vs OLS 0.181+/-0.078).
core_contribution: The paper develops a statistical (not security/systems) framework for machine unlearning under generic differentiable loss functions. For squared loss it derives a closed-form 'Unlearning Least Squares' (ULS) estimator that fine-tunes a pre-trained model using only the forget data and a small random subsample of the remaining data (not the full remaining dataset). The authors prove: (1) finite-sample convergence rates for ULS (Theorem 3.1) and its gradient-descent realization (Theorem 3.2); (2) a minimax lower bound (Theorem 3.3) showing ULS attains the information-theoretic optimal rate when the 'unlearning cost' term omega_f*delta <= 1; (3) a robustified variant ULS+ (Theorem 5.1/5.2) that is minimax optimal even when omega_f*delta >> 1; and (4) asymptotically valid inference (confidence intervals) for linear functionals of the remaining-data parameter (Theorem 4.1, Lemma 4.2), without requiring full retraining. They also give a formal convergence-rate analysis (Theorem 5.3) showing that GradDiff, a heuristic used in LLM unlearning, is NOT minimax optimal when the forget-proportion-times-discrepancy term is small, whereas ULS is.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is a statistical estimation-theory paper, not an attack/defense paper. The 'threat' motivating the work is regulatory/compliance (GDPR Article 17 'right to be forgotten', CCPA) and bias correction (removing outliers/contaminated data), not an adversary with a defined capability or attack surface. No adversarial model, attacker capabilities, or security guarantee (e.g., certified removal / differential privacy bound) is defined or proven in the main text; the paper explicitly distinguishes its focus (bias-correction/outlier-removal unlearning) from the privacy/indistinguishability formalization of unlearning (attributed to Izzo et al. 2021, Sekhari et al. 2021, Neel et al. 2021) and states it does NOT adopt that privacy-oriented definition.
limitations: The method requires smoothness (differentiability, and for the closed-form ULS, quadratic/squared loss) of the loss function; the authors explicitly state that extending to non-smooth optimization landscapes (e.g., Lasso or quantile regression) is a substantial open technical challenge, as is extension to nonparametric models and deep neural networks. The theory assumes sub-Gaussian, independent (though not identically distributed) data and low-dimensional-relative-to-sample-size regimes (p much smaller than min{N_f, tilde n_r}, and p^2 = o(min{tilde n_r, N_f}) for the inference results). The paper's own framing explicitly excludes the privacy/indistinguishability notion of unlearning (i.e., it does not aim for or prove any certified-removal or differential-privacy-style guarantee) — it targets bias correction/outlier removal only, so it should not be read as providing a privacy or security guarantee for LLM unlearning despite discussing GradDiff and connecting to LLM unlearning heuristics.

datasets_used: Simulated linear-model data (Gaussian remaining-data covariates N(0,Ip); AR(1)-correlated forget-data covariates with (Sigma_f)_{j,k}=0.3^|j-k|), Nr=20000, Nf in {1000,2000}
Yelp review public dataset (6M+ reviews; 200,000-review random sample used; Nr=129144, Nf=38569; bag-of-words p=1500 features from 1500 most frequent words)
UK Biobank hospital episode records from 2022 (7,602 records after cleaning; Nr=5619, Nf=577; p=16 categorical predictors; response = log10(length of stay))
quantitative_results: Simulation inference (Table 1, Nr=20000, Nf=1000): ULS 95% CI coverage 0.935-0.960 across tilde n_r/N_r in {0.1,0.2,0.3}, p in {10,50,100}, delta in {1,2,3}; OLS coverage 0.942-0.960
Simulation inference average SD (x10^-2): ULS 0.72-0.81 vs OLS 1.30-2.26 across the same settings

Yelp running time per estimator (Table 2, seconds): retrained 0.44, PRU 90.08, GradDiff 25.60 (incl. CV), OLS 0.12, ULS 0.25, ULS+ 25.20 (incl. CV) — PRU reported as ~300x slower than other estimators

UK Biobank 95% CIs for 'operative procedures' coefficient (Table 3): ULS 0.158+/-0.029, 0.163+/-0.026, 0.159+/-0.025 vs OLS 0.181+/-0.078, 0.172+/-0.053, 0.147+/-0.044 for tilde n_r/N_r = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 respectively
Theorem 3.1: ULS estimation error <= C*sqrt(p/Nr) + C*omega_f*delta*sqrt(p/tilde n_r) w.p. >= 1-exp(-c1*p)

Theorem 5.3: GradDiff error, with optimally tuned lambda, converges only to O(sqrt(p/tilde n_r)), matching subsampled OLS rate (i.e. provably not minimax optimal for the unlearning problem when omega_f*delta = o(1))
baselines_compared: Retrained estimator theta_hat_r^(rtr) (oracle, full retraining on remaining data only)
Subsampled OLS tilde theta_r^(ols) (least squares on the small remaining subsample only)
GradDiff theta_hat_r^(diff) (Liu et al. 2022/2025a gradient-difference LLM-unlearning heuristic)
Projective Residual Update (PRU) theta_hat_r^(pru) (Izzo et al. 2021, real-data experiments only)
